title: Behold and Do
subtitle: On the threshold of the Passion: Christ, the pattern shown on the mountain

stillness: Be still now, and turn the eye of your heart inward.
We come to the threshold of the Lord's Passion.
Let us sprinkle our book, and the doorposts of our houses, with the blood.
gospel: Look, and make according to the pattern that was shown to you on the mountain.
that you should follow in his footsteps.
I judged myself to know nothing among you,
save Jesus Christ, and him crucified.
behold: Now we draw near to the Passion of the Lord,
which we ought both to gaze upon with love and to imitate in deed.
For Christ is like a book set before us as a pattern,
and to the likeness of that pattern we must shape and correct our whole life.
Though Christ is often called a mountain in Scripture, for the height of his perfection,
most of all is he called a mountain lifted up upon the cross, for the sublimity of his most sacred Passion.
On this mountain, then — Christ crucified — the pattern is shown us,
to be diligently gazed upon and effectually imitated.
For it is not enough for a Christian to gaze upon Christ suffering —
this the Jews and the Gentiles did, his very crucifiers —
but it is required also to work and to do according to the pattern shown.
Behold from the beginning of his Nativity how poor he was born,
who had neither home nor garments,
but, born in a mean lodging, was laid in a manger upon a little hay,
before the brute beasts, wrapped in poor swaddling-bands.
On the eighth day he was circumcised, and already began to pour out his blood for us.
Behold how, in the time of great cold, he willed to be plunged and baptized in the icy waters,
and how, through forty days of unbroken fasting, he was worn away,
and how many temptations he then endured from the devil.
Behold with how great toil he lived,
preaching daily in the temple, and from city to city,
often passing the whole night in prayer.
Behold how his whole life was in labors and sufferings.
consider: Consider this: not only whatever good Christ did on earth,
but whatever evil he endured — all of it was the schooling of our conduct.
The Passion of the Lord embraces in itself every perfection possible to man in this life.
On the cross of the Lord is the end of the Law and of Scripture;
in his Passion, the sum of all perfection;
in his death, the consummation of every word.
If poverty be spoken of — who was ever poorer than Christ, hanging naked on the cross,
who had not where to lay his head?
If obedience and humility — who ever so humbled and emptied himself,
made obedient unto death, even the death of the cross?
If charity — who ever had so great a love as Christ,
who laid down his soul for his sheep, and willed to suffer such horrors for us,
when he could have freed us by a single nod of his will?
By his most shameful death, we have a remedy against pride;
because he joined himself to thieves, a remedy against envy;
because he kept silent before those who blasphemed him, a remedy against anger;
by his nakedness and poverty, a remedy against avarice;
by his tasting of gall and vinegar, a remedy against gluttony;
by the infliction of his wounds, a remedy against lust.
These are the seven seals he opened for us in his Passion,
and so unlocked for us the entrance into life.
Who ever prayed more mightily than Christ,
who from the vehement strain of his prayer sweated drops of blood?
prayer: who on the third day before the time of your Passion and death
foretold to your disciples your Pasch and your passing out of this world,
grant me, through all the time of my life,
to live in the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth;
and let me not pass out of this world
that is, through contrition of heart, confession of mouth, and satisfaction of deed —
you blot out all my sins,
granting me the communion of your most sacred Body and Blood,
the anointing of holy oil, and in all things a perfect consummation;
and at the last, happily consummated,
make me pass over to you, my most sweet Lord, blessed above all things. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Itaque in cruce Domini est finis Legis atque Scripturæ, in Passione ejus summa omnis perfectionis, in morte ipsius est consummatio omnis sermonis.
latinJewel.english: And so on the cross of the Lord is the end of the Law and of Scripture; in his Passion, the sum of all perfection; in his death, the consummation of every word.
